There are two main types of mountain bike  disc brakes: mechanical disc brakes and hydraulic disc brakes.

Mechanical disc brakes use a brake cable housed in a brake housing, which connects the brake lever to the brake caliper. When the lever is pulled, it tightens the cable, which in turn actuates the caliper and presses the brake pads against the rotor.

Hydraulic disc brakes, on the other hand, use a sealed hydraulic system instead of a cable. When the brake lever is pulled, it pressurizes the incompressible brake fluid (typically mineral oil or DOT fluid), which forces the brake pistons to move the pads against the rotor. Hydraulic systems generally provide stronger and more consistent braking performance compared to mechanical brakes.

Typical features of a mountain bike are very wide tires. The original 26-inch (660 mm) wheel diameter with approximately 2.125-inch (54.0 mm) width (ISO 559 mm rim diameter) is increasingly being displaced by 29-inch (740 mm) wheels with 2.35-inch (60 mm) width (ISO 622 mm rim diameter), as well as the 27.5-inch (700 mm) wheel diameter with 2.25-inch (57 mm) widths (ISO 584 mm rim diameter), particularly on smaller frame sizes for shorter riders. Mountain bikes with 24-inch (610 mm) wheels are also available, sometimes for dirt jumping, or as a junior bike.

In some countries, mainly in Continental Europe, 700C (622 mm) wheels are commonly called 28-inch wheels. 24-inch (610 mm) wheels are used for dirt jumping bikes and sometimes on freeride bikes, rear wheel only, as this makes the bike more maneuverable. 29-inch (740 mm) wheels were once used for only cross country purposes, but are now becoming more commonplace in other disciplines of mountain biking. Wheels come in a variety of widths, ranging from standard rims suitable for use with tires in the 1.90 to 2.10 in (48 to 53 mm) size, to 2.35 and 3.00 in (60 and 76 mm) widths popular with freeride and downhill bicycles. Although heavier wheelsets are favored in the freeride and downhill disciplines, advances in wheel technology continually shave weight off strong wheels. This is highly advantageous as rolling weight greatly affects handling and control, which are very important to the technical nature of freeride and downhill riding.

Manufacturers produce bicycle tires with a wide variety of tread patterns to suit different needs. Among these styles are: slick street tires, street tires with a center ridge and outer tread, fully knobby, front-specific, rear-specific, and snow studded. Some tires can be specifically designed for use in certain weather (wet or dry) and terrain (hard, soft, muddy, etc.) conditions. Other tire designs attempt to be all-around applicable. Within the same intended application, more expensive tires tend to be lighter and have less rolling resistance. Sticky rubber tires are now available for use on freeride and downhill bikes. Tires also come with tubes, tubeless and tubeless-ready. Tires with tubes are the standard design and the easiest to use and maintain. Tubeless tires are significantly lighter and often have better performance because you can run them at a lower tire pressure which results in better traction and increasing rolling resistance. Tubeless-ready tires are tires that can use tubes or go tubeless. A liquid sealant is used without the tube to secure the seal to the rim. Popular tire manufacturers include Wilderness Trail Bikes, Schwalbe, Maxxis, Nokian, Michelin, Continental, Tioga, Kenda, Hutchinson, Specialized and Panaracer.

Mountain bikes are available in tandem configurations. For example, Cannondale and Santana Cycles offer ones without suspension, while Ellsworth, Nicolai, and Ventana manufacture tandems with full suspension.

Vintage MTB Day is an event or gathering where enthusiasts celebrate and ride classic, older mountain bikes (MTBs). The occasion typically features bicycles from the 1970s, 1980s, and early 1990s, showcasing the evolution of mountain bike technology and design. The event was established to honor and recognize the pioneers of mountain biking.
Held every 13 August in cities and countries around the world, Vintage MTB Day is open to participants of all backgrounds and encourages community involvement through group rides, exhibitions, and discussions of vintage bicycle culture.

Across different countries, Vintage MTB Day is marked by events focused on classic mountain bikes. Common activities include:
Fun Ride & Retro Gathering – group rides where participants use vintage MTBs and wear retro cycling gear.
Bike Exhibitions – collectors display frames, components, and accessories such as cantilever brakes, thumb shifters, and semi-slick tires from the 1980s.
Workshops & Talks – sessions on restoring old bikes, mountain biking history, and maintaining classic components.
Social Media Campaigns – sharing photos or videos of vintage MTBs with the hashtag #VintageMTBDay.

Beyond nostalgia, the celebration promotes sustainability by encouraging the restoration and continued use of older bicycles. Vintage MTB Day inspires a new generation of cyclists to learn about mountain biking history, reduce waste through refurbishing, and preserve cycling culture across generations.

metadata.title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks

text:
Mud, dirt, and trail debris wear down components like chains, brakes, and suspension, causing expensive repairs if neglected. Well-maintained bikes deliver reliable handling and extended component lifespan. Your MTB always signals when it needs attention — squeaks, slips, and wobbles are its language.

---

metadata.title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks

text:
### 1. Rinse the Bike (3 min)
Use a garden hose with moderate pressure — avoid pressure washers, which force water past seals. Rinse top to bottom:
- Frame and fork
- Shock and wheels
- Drivetrain
- Underside of downtube
- Linkage areas (full-suspension bikes)
- Bottom bracket zone

For caked mud, let water soak briefly before rinsing. A soft brush helps dislodge stubborn debris.

### 2. Chain Clean and Lube (4 min)

A dirty chain shifts poorly, wears faster, and damages cassettes and chainrings.

**Quick clean:** Run a clean rag around the chain while backpedalling.

**Deeper clean:** Use a chain cleaning device with degreaser every few rides.

metadata.title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks

text:
**Quick clean:** Run a clean rag around the chain while backpedalling.

**Deeper clean:** Use a chain cleaning device with degreaser every few rides.

**Lubrication:** Apply one drop of lube per link while slowly backpedalling. Backpedal a dozen times to work it in, then wipe excess with a clean rag.
**Lube selection lifehack:**
- Wet lube for wet/muddy conditions — stays longer but attracts dirt
- Dry lube for dry conditions — sheds dirt better but washes off easily
- Less is more: too much lube just attracts more grit

### 3. Brake Pad Inspection (2 min)

### 3. Brake Pad Inspection (2 min)
Look through the top of each brake caliper. Check friction material thickness — most pads have a wear indicator groove.
- Replace when worn to 1–1.5 mm remaining
- Check rotor scoring by running a finger lightly along the braking surface when cool
- Spongy lever or lever reaching the bar = worn pads or air in the hydraulic system

### 4. Tyre Inspection (2 min)

metadata.title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks

text:

- Spongy lever or lever reaching the bar = worn pads or air in the hydraulic system

### 4. Tyre Inspection (2 min)
Run hands around tread and sidewall:
- **Embedded debris:** Remove thorns, flints, or wire fragments immediately
- **Cuts and gashes:** Deep cuts exposing the casing need replacement soon
- **Sidewall condition:** Bulging sidewalls = compromised integrity
- **Knob wear:** Excessively worn knobs reduce grip in mud and corners
- Check pressure with a gauge — tubeless tyres lose 1–2 PSI between rides, especially in cold weather

### 5. Bolt Check (2 min)

metadata.title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks

text:

- Check pressure with a gauge — tubeless tyres lose 1–2 PSI between rides, especially in cold weather

### 5. Bolt Check (2 min)
Quick squeeze and wiggle — not a full re-torque, just confirming nothing loosened:
- Handlebar and stem bolts
- Wheel axles (lift and rock side-to-side)
- Saddle and seatpost / dropper collar
- Brake caliper mounts
- Crank bolts (rock each arm laterally)

**Lifehack:** Use a torque wrench for stem bolts and carbon components — over-tightening damages aluminum and carbon.

### 6. Suspension Wipe (2 min)

Wipe exposed fork stanchion tubes with a clean, soft cloth. Remove dirt and grit that would otherwise drag past seals into fork internals. Do the same for the rear shock shaft. Weeping oil or drips = seal failure needing attention.

---

metadata.title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks

text:
- **Drivetrain assessment:** Check for shark-fin-shaped teeth on chainring or cassette (sign of wear). Use a chain wear tool — replace chain at 0.5–0.75% wear and your cassette/chainring will last 2–3x longer.
- **Wheel check:** Spin each wheel and watch rim alignment relative to brake pads. Squeeze pairs of spokes — all should feel similar in tension.
- **Suspension air pressure:** Fork pressure deflates naturally over time — check bi-weekly.
- **Pivot bearings (full suspension):** Rock rear wheel side-to-side while holding frame. Any knocking or play beyond the hub = worn pivot bearings.
- **Inspect brake pads:** Replace if thinner than 1–2 mm.
- **Torque bolts:** Go through all fasteners with a torque wrench.

text:
| Frequency | Tasks |

|---|---|

| After every ride | Rinse bike, wipe chain, apply light lube, check tire pressure, tighten axles, wipe suspension stanchions |

| Every 3–5 rides | Inspect brake pads, check suspension air pressure, measure chain wear, torque bolts, inspect tires |

| Every 50 hours | Lower leg fork service: clean/re-grease bushings, replace dust seals and foam rings, refill oil |

| Every 3–6 months | Service fork lowers, bleed brakes if needed, deep-clean drivetrain, refresh tubeless sealant, inspect frame |

| Every 100–150 hours | Full suspension service: fork damper, rear shock, brake bleed, headset, bottom bracket, hubs, drivetrain |

| Every 6–12 months | Service rear shock, full frame inspection (bearings, headset, bottom bracket) |

metadata.title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks

text:
- **Track your ride hours** using a GPS unit or app (Strava, TrailForks) to schedule service based on actual use, not just calendar time.

- **Avoid high-pressure washers** — they force water past seals and into bearings.

- **Store your bike indoors** or in a sheltered area. Use hooks for elevation. UV, rain, and humidity cause rust and deterioration.

- **Tubeless sealant dries out** — top it up every 2–3 months or after a puncture repair.

- **Less chain lube is more** — the goal is lubrication inside the chain links, not a shiny coat outside.

- **Use manufacturer-recommended products** for suspension lubrication — wrong products degrade seals.

- **Lockout compression** should only be used on roads or smooth singletrack — active suspension helps traction on climbs too.

metadata.title: Mountain Bike Maintenance: Tips, Intervals & Lifehacks

text:

- **Lockout compression** should only be used on roads or smooth singletrack — active suspension helps traction on climbs too.

- **Build the 15-minute habit** consistently — small regular effort dramatically extends component life and reduces costly repairs.

- **When to go to a shop:** Persistent gear skipping after cleaning, suspension leaks, wobbly wheels or bent rotors that resist DIY fixes.

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension

text:
Riding a full-suspension mountain bike instead of a hardtail generally allows you to traverse technical terrain with less effort and maintain better traction on rough or loose surfaces—but only with proper suspension setup. Poor suspension configuration is a primary reason many riders experience suboptimal performance on their bikes.

### Suspension Terms

**Travel**: The amount of suspension compression available, measured in millimeters. For example, the Liv Hail offers 160mm while the Pique provides 120mm.

**Fork**: Front suspension on a mountain bike.

**Shock**: Rear suspension on a mountain bike.

**Stanchion**: The polished component of the fork or shock that extends into the fork lowers or shock body.

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension

text:
ck**: Rear suspension on a mountain bike.

**Stanchion**: The polished component of the fork or shock that extends into the fork lowers or shock body.

**Sag**: The travel consumed simply by sitting on the bike. A sag test measures adequate air pressure in your fork or shock. Ideally, you should compress 25-35% of total travel when seated.

**Shock Pump**: The specialized device for pressurizing suspension components. Standard bike tire pumps are incompatible.

**Compression Damping**: A control knob on some forks and shocks regulating oil flow through internal ports. Greater compression damping produces a firmer feel.

**Rebound Damping**: This adjustment controls how quickly your fork or shock returns to its resting position. Increased rebound damping slows the return speed.

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension

text:
1. Recruit a friend to stabilize your front wheel during testing.

2. If your suspension includes compression damping knobs, position them fully open.

3. Stand on the pedals and bounce several times while applying slight front-end pressure, mimicking riding conditions. This mobilizes the internal oil and prevents stiction.

4. While maintaining your typical riding position, have your assistant reset the rubber o-rings on your stanchion.

5. Gently lower yourself onto the saddle, then dismount.

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension
metadata.section: How to Set Sag on a Mountain Bike

text:
our typical riding position, have your assistant reset the rubber o-rings on your stanchion.

5. Gently lower yourself onto the saddle, then dismount.

6. Examine your stanchion. The o-ring should sit approximately one-quarter down from the top. If it's positioned less than one-quarter down, you have excessive air pressure—use your shock pump to release air. If positioned more than one-quarter down, you lack sufficient pressure—add air accordingly.

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension
metadata.section: How to Set Sag on a Mountain Bike

text: After confirming correct sag, proceed to rebound adjustment. Rebound tuning remains highly personal, yet improper settings significantly compromise riding quality. As a starting point, determine your total available clicks by rotating the rebound knob fully counterclockwise (toward the jackalope symbol on RockShox or minus sign on Fox) to eliminate all damping. Count clicks while rotating clockwise (toward the turtle on RockShox or plus sign on Fox). Return halfway through your count.

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension

text:
o eliminate all damping. Count clicks while rotating clockwise (toward the turtle on RockShox or plus sign on Fox). Return halfway through your count.

Test the rebound feel by compressing and releasing either the saddle (for shock rebound) or handlebars (for fork rebound) using open-palm pressure without gripping. Your suspension should return promptly to center without bouncing excessively. Adjust clicks as needed. "Slower" rebound requires additional damping (turtle/plus), while "faster" rebound needs less damping (jackalope/minus).

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension

text:
ely. Adjust clicks as needed. "Slower" rebound requires additional damping (turtle/plus), while "faster" rebound needs less damping (jackalope/minus).

Fine-tuning happens best during actual riding. If your bike feels like it's launching you on landings or the ride feels harsh over technical features, reduce rebound speed. If the suspension "packs up" over consecutive bumps and fails returning to neutral, increase rebound speed.

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension

text:
Every fork and shock differs in damping options—some offer high and low-speed compression settings, others provide two or three options, and some have none. Review your specific suspension manufacturer's documentation and recommendations.

One key consideration: if your suspension provides a lockout compression position, use it exclusively on roads, gravel, or smooth singletrack. With correctly configured sag, maintain active suspension while climbing technical terrain. Suspension assists traction when ascending rocks and roots just as it does descending—keeping tires grounded improves traction throughout.

metadata.title: How to Set Up Mountain Bike Suspension

text:
al terrain. Suspension assists traction when ascending rocks and roots just as it does descending—keeping tires grounded improves traction throughout.

Suspension setup involves considerable technical depth. The primary advice: experiment with your fork and shock adjustments. Learn what each control does and discover your preferred settings through hands-on testing.
